title: Izakaya Dialogue
subtitle: Welcome & First Drinks
sceneLabel: Night Out / Izakaya with Overseas Team Members

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Welcome to Japan! I'm glad you both made it safely. This is a classic izakaya — kind of like a Japanese pub.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.text: Wow, it looks amazing in here! I've always wanted to try a real izakaya. What do you recommend?

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.text: Same here. I'm not sure what to order. Can you help us out?

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Of course! Let's start with some drinks. Do you prefer beer, sake, or maybe a non-alcoholic option?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.text: I'll go with a beer. When in Rome, right? Or I guess, when in Japan!

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.text: I'd like to try sake if that's okay. I've heard it goes really well with Japanese food.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Great choices! Let's order some food too. I'd suggest the yakitori, edamame, and karaage to start. They're all pretty popular.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.text: Sounds good to me. By the way, what is karaage exactly? Is it similar to fried chicken?

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: Yes, exactly! It's Japanese fried chicken, usually marinated in soy sauce and ginger. You'll love it, I promise.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.text: That sounds delicious. I should mention that I don't eat red meat. Is that going to be a problem here?

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: No problem at all! We have plenty of options — tofu, grilled fish, and lots of vegetables. I'll let the server know.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.text: This is so much fun. I really appreciate you taking the time to show us around, Yuki.

dialogue.speaker: Yuki
dialogue.text: My pleasure! Okay, the drinks are here. Let's raise our glasses — cheers, or as we say in Japanese, Kanpai!

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.text: Kanpai! This is going to be a great trip.

dialogue.speaker: Sara
dialogue.text: Kanpai! Thank you so much, Yuki. We're really happy to be here.

vocab.desc: 〜をおすすめします。食事・観光・仕事など、何かを提案するときに使える万能フレーズ。
助けてもらえますか？help me よりも自然でカジュアルな頼み方。チームでの会話にもぴったり。
郷に入れば郷に従え。その場の文化や慣習に合わせるときに使うユーモアのある表現。
〜をお伝えしておきたいのですが。食事制限やアレルギーを丁寧に伝えるときに便利。
全然大丈夫です！相手の心配を払拭する気持ちのいい一言。Don't worry よりも積極的なニュアンス。
〜に伝えておきます。店員に言っておくなど幅広い場面で使える。
どういたしまして / 喜んで！You're welcome よりも温かみのある返し方。
乾杯しましょう。Cheers! の前に使うと、より丁寧でパーティらしい雰囲気が出る。

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Okay, the menu is here. There's a lot to choose from. Do you want me to walk you through it?

dialogue.speaker: Mike
dialogue.text: Yes, please! I can't read the kanji, so I'm a little lost. What's good here?

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: This place is known for its grilled skewers — yakitori. We can get a mix of chicken, vegetables, and mushrooms.

dialogue.speaker: Emma
dialogue.text: Oh, that sounds great. Are the portions small? I want to try as many dishes as possible.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Yes, they're meant for sharing. That's the best part of an izakaya — you order a little bit of everything.

dialogue.speaker: Mike
dialogue.text: I love that idea. Let's go for it. Should we get another round of drinks too?

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Definitely. Excuse me! Could we get two more beers and one more sake, please?

dialogue.speaker: Emma
dialogue.text: Kenji, what's that dish over there? The one that looks crispy. It smells amazing.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: That's takoyaki — octopus balls. They're a street food originally from Osaka. Want to give it a try?

dialogue.speaker: Emma
dialogue.text: Octopus? That's a bit out of my comfort zone, but sure, I'll give it a shot!

dialogue.speaker: Mike
dialogue.text: Ha, good for you! I'll try it too. How do you eat it? Just pick it up?

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: Yes, use the toothpick. And be careful — they're really hot inside. Blow on it first.

dialogue.speaker: Emma
dialogue.text: Wow, this is actually really good! I can't believe I almost said no to this.

dialogue.speaker: Mike
dialogue.text: Agreed! Kenji, you have to come visit us next time. We'll take you out for dinner too.

dialogue.speaker: Kenji
dialogue.text: I'd love that! Alright, the yakitori is coming. Let's dig in!

vocab.phrase: Do you want me to walk you through it?
vocab.desc: 一緒に説明しましょうか？メニュー・資料など、何かを順番に案内するときの定番フレーズ。
ちょっとわからなくて困っています。道だけでなく、説明が理解できないときにも使える自然な表現。
シェアするためのものです。〜はこういう目的で作られていると説明するときに便利。
〜はどうしますか？グループで何かを決めるときに使う、やわらかい提案の言い方。

vocab.phrase: That's a bit out of my comfort zone.
vocab.desc: ちょっと慣れていないことで。新しいことに挑戦するときの素直な気持ちを伝えるフレーズ。
やってみます！I'll try it よりも元気でポジティブなニュアンス。

vocab.phrase: I can't believe I almost said no to this.
vocab.desc: 断らなくてよかった！予想外においしかったときの感動を伝える表現。
さあ、食べよう！食事が来たときのいただきます的なカジュアルな表現。

title: Wrapping Up the Night
subtitle: Bill, Karaoke & Goodbye
sceneLabel: Izakaya / Paying the Bill & Saying Goodbye

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.text: That was such a great meal. I think I ate way too much though!

dialogue.speaker: Lily
dialogue.text: Same! The food just kept coming. Hana, this place was such a good pick. Thank you for bringing us here.

dialogue.speaker: Hana
dialogue.text: I'm so glad you enjoyed it! This is one of my favorite spots. Shall we ask for the bill?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.text: Yes, let's do that. By the way, how does paying work here? Do we split it, or is it all together?

dialogue.speaker: Hana
dialogue.text: Either works. We can split it evenly if you want. But tonight is on me — you're our guests!

dialogue.speaker: Lily
dialogue.text: Oh, that's really kind of you, but we can't let you pay for everything. Let us at least cover the drinks.

dialogue.speaker: Hana
dialogue.text: Don't worry about it! Next time you're in town, I'll let you treat me. Deal?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.text: Deal! You've got yourself a free dinner next time you visit us. We'll take good care of you.

dialogue.speaker: Hana
dialogue.text: I'll hold you to that! Okay, I paid. Ready to head out?

dialogue.speaker: Lily
dialogue.text: Yes, but I don't want the night to end! What do people usually do after izakaya?

dialogue.speaker: Hana
dialogue.text: Some people go to a karaoke bar! It's right around the corner. Are you up for it?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.text: Karaoke?! I haven't done that in years. Count me in!

dialogue.speaker: Lily
dialogue.text: Fair warning — I'm a terrible singer. But I'll give it my best shot!

dialogue.speaker: Hana
dialogue.text: Ha! That's the spirit. Nobody cares how well you sing at karaoke. Let's go have fun!

vocab.desc: 〜しましょうか？Should we ~? よりもやや丁寧で自然な提案表現。
今夜は私のおごりです。I'll pay よりもカジュアルで温かい言い方。

vocab.phrase: We can't let you pay for everything.
vocab.desc: 全部払わせるわけにはいきません。相手の厚意を断って費用を分担しようとするときの丁寧な断り方。
約束ですよ？提案に合意を求めるカジュアルな一言。相手も Deal! と返すのがセット。
その言葉、忘れませんよ！約束を冗談っぽく念押しするときの表現。
やる気ある？何かに誘うときのカジュアルな聞き方。
参加します！自分も加えてほしいと積極的に伝える定番フレーズ。
その意気です！相手が前向きな姿勢を見せたときに使う励ましの言葉。

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.text: You know, I've been coming to Japan for five years now, but this is my first time in a place like this — no English menu, no tourist photos. This is the real deal.

dialogue.speaker: Natsuki
dialogue.text: Ha! Most tourists don't even know this neighborhood exists. I'm glad you're open to this kind of place.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Honestly, every time I visit Japan, I find something new that I love. Last time it was sento — public baths. This time I want to try more local food spots like this one.

dialogue.speaker: Natsuki
dialogue.text: I love that you already know about sento! Most people skip those. What drew you to it?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: A colleague back home mentioned it. She said it's less about bathing and more about slowing down. She was totally right — I sat in that hot water for an hour and forgot about everything.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.text: That's what gets me about Japan. There's this idea of doing one thing really well — whether it's a bowl of ramen or a cup of tea. It's almost like a philosophy.

dialogue.speaker: Natsuki
dialogue.text: You're thinking of shokunin culture — the idea that a craftsman devotes their life to mastering one skill. It shows up in everything from sushi chefs to carpenters.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.text: Yes, exactly! That concept really resonates with me. I think about it a lot in my own work — am I going deep enough, or just spreading myself too thin?

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: That's such a good question. I feel the same pressure back home — do more, move faster. Being here kind of puts things in perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Natsuki
dialogue.text: I never thought about it from the outside before. It's interesting to hear how Japan looks from a foreigner's point of view. You notice things we take for granted.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.text: Like the way everyone lines up perfectly at the train station. Or how the food here came out in exactly the right order without us asking. Small things, but they add up.

dialogue.speaker: Natsuki
dialogue.text: We call that omotenashi — hospitality that anticipates your needs before you even say anything. I'm happy it still shows up in places like this.

dialogue.speaker: Priya
dialogue.text: Omotenashi. I'm going to remember that word. Honestly, I think Japan has made me a better host back home. I pay much more attention to the little details now.

dialogue.speaker: Natsuki
dialogue.text: That might be the best thing I've heard all night. Okay — more drinks? I think we've earned another round.

vocab.desc: これこそ本物だ。本格的なものに出会ったときの感嘆フレーズ。

vocab.phrase: That concept really resonates with me.
vocab.desc: そのコンセプト、すごく共感します。I agree よりも深い共鳴・感動を伝える知的な表現。
あれもこれもやりすぎて手が回らない状態。仕事の優先順位について話すときに使える慣用表現。
物事を俯瞰できるようになる・視野が広がる。体験によって大切なことが見えてきたときに使う。
当たり前だと思っていること。慣れすぎて気づかなくなっているものを指す定番フレーズ。

vocab.phrase: Small things, but they add up.
vocab.desc: 小さなことだけど、積み重なると大きい。細部へのこだわりの価値を伝えるときに使える。

vocab.phrase: I pay much more attention to ~ now.
vocab.desc: 今は〜にずっと気を配るようになった。体験を通じて変わった自分の習慣を伝えるときに使える。

vocab.phrase: I think we've earned another round.
vocab.desc: もう一杯飲む資格があると思う（笑）。もう一杯を冗談っぽく提案するユーモアのある言い回し。

title: Hidden Gems & Tips
subtitle: Local Trains, Conbini & Japan Hangover
sceneLabel: Izakaya / Insider Tips & Life in Japan

dialogue.speaker: Mei
dialogue.text: Oh, that's easy. Take a local train out of the city — not the shinkansen, just a regular slow train — and get off somewhere random. That's where the real Japan is.

dialogue.speaker: Jordan
dialogue.text: That's exactly the kind of thing I'm after. I feel like I've done the big cities — Tokyo, Kyoto. I want something more off the beaten path this time.

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.text: I did something like that last year — ended up in a tiny town that wasn't in any guidebook. Found this incredible family-run soba restaurant. I still think about it.

dialogue.speaker: Mei
dialogue.text: Have you been to the Noto Peninsula? It's stunning and still pretty quiet. People there are so warm.

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.text: I've heard about that. It's on my list for sure. By the way, Mei — what do you miss most when you're not in Japan?

dialogue.speaker: Mei
dialogue.text: Honestly? The convenience stores. I know that sounds funny, but a good conbini at midnight when you're tired — there's nothing like it anywhere else in the world.

dialogue.speaker: Jordan
dialogue.text: Ha! That actually makes complete sense. I grabbed a chicken sandwich from 7-Eleven here last night, and it was honestly better than most cafes back home.

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.text: The bar is just higher here for everyday things. I think that's what keeps drawing me back — the standard of care in small things is just different.

dialogue.speaker: Mei
dialogue.text: You both get it. A lot of visitors never quite put their finger on it, but that's exactly what it is. It's not one big thing — it's thousands of small ones done right.

dialogue.speaker: Jordan
dialogue.text: That's why I always feel a little restless when I get back home. Like something's slightly off. Does that sound dramatic?

dialogue.speaker: Sofia
dialogue.text: Not at all. I call it the Japan hangover. You come back and everything feels a little noisier and a little messier for a while.

dialogue.speaker: Mei
dialogue.text: The Japan hangover — I love that. You know, hearing you both say this makes me really proud. I think I needed the reminder to appreciate where I'm from.

dialogue.speaker: Jordan
dialogue.text: Well, we're very grateful to have a local guide. Same time next year? I'm already looking forward to it.

dialogue.speaker: Mei
dialogue.text: You don't even have to ask. I'll keep a seat warm for you right here at this very table.

vocab.phrase: What's the one thing I should ~ that most people miss?
vocab.desc: ほとんどの人が見逃す、絶対やるべきことは何ですか？地元の人におすすめを聞くときの定番フレーズ。
人があまり行かない場所・観光地以外のところ。ガイドブックに載っていないような穴場を探すときに使う。
それがまさに求めているものです。提案がぴったりはまったときに使える自然な返し方。

vocab.phrase: The bar is just higher here.
vocab.desc: ここは基準・水準が高い。品質やサービスのレベルを比較して語るときに使えるシンプルな表現。

vocab.phrase: Never quite put their finger on it
vocab.desc: うまく言葉にできない。何となく感じているが言語化できないものを表現するときに使う。
大げさに聞こえますか？自分の感情が強すぎないか確認する、会話を和らげるための一言。
〜することを改めて気づかせてもらいました。他者の言葉から大切なことを再認識したときの感謝の表現。

vocab.phrase: I'll keep a seat warm for you.
vocab.desc: いつでも待っています。また来てほしいという温かい気持ちを伝える比喩的な表現。

dialogue.speaker: Dan
dialogue.text: Okay, I have to ask — we're sitting here in Japan, in an izakaya, drinking sake. Did anyone else feel like they just walked into an anime scene?

dialogue.speaker: Aisha
dialogue.text: Oh, one hundred percent. I kept thinking of Shirobako earlier today — all those little side streets reminded me of the backgrounds in the show.

dialogue.speaker: Tomoya
dialogue.text: Ha! I love that you know Shirobako. That's not a title most overseas fans pick up. How did you find it?

dialogue.speaker: Aisha
dialogue.text: A friend recommended it after I said I wanted to understand how anime is actually made. It completely changed how I watch everything now — I notice so much more detail.

dialogue.speaker: Dan
dialogue.text: Same for me with Ping Pong the Animation. I didn't expect a sports anime to hit that hard emotionally. The art style threw me off at first, but by episode three I was totally hooked.

dialogue.speaker: Tomoya
dialogue.text: That's a classic. Masaaki Yuasa has a way of making you feel things you didn't expect. His work doesn't look like typical anime, but that's exactly the point.

dialogue.speaker: Aisha
dialogue.text: Can I ask something? I've always been curious — do people here actually watch anime, or is it more of an overseas thing?

dialogue.speaker: Tomoya
dialogue.text: It's a fair question. Plenty of people here watch it, but some treat it as a niche thing — kind of like how comic book fans are seen in some countries. It really depends on who you ask.

dialogue.speaker: Dan
dialogue.text: That's interesting. Back home, anime has gone pretty mainstream. My younger cousins who don't even know Japan well can name five Ghibli films off the top of their head.

dialogue.speaker: Tomoya
dialogue.text: Ghibli is its own thing, really. My grandmother loves Totoro. It cuts across age groups in a way that most anime doesn't. It's more like cinema than animation to a lot of people here.

dialogue.speaker: Aisha
dialogue.text: That's such a good way to put it. I think what draws me to anime is that it takes storytelling seriously. It doesn't talk down to the audience the way some Western animation does.

dialogue.speaker: Dan
dialogue.text: Totally agree. And the emotional range is just wider. A show can be funny, heartbreaking, and philosophical all in one episode. It keeps you on your toes.

dialogue.speaker: Tomoya
dialogue.text: It makes me really happy to hear that. Since we're on the topic, what's your number one recommendation for someone new to anime?

dialogue.speaker: Aisha
dialogue.text: Tough call, but I'd say A Silent Voice. It's emotional, beautifully made, and it doesn't need any background knowledge to connect with it.

dialogue.speaker: Dan
dialogue.text: Mine would be Spirited Away — it drops you into a world with no explanation and trusts you to keep up. That's still one of the bravest things I've ever seen in any kind of film.

dialogue.speaker: Tomoya
dialogue.text: Both great picks. You know, this conversation is making me want to rewatch everything. Let's order one more round and keep going.

vocab.desc: 完全同意！Absolutely や Totally と同じ意味で、強く同意するときのカジュアルな返し方。

vocab.phrase: It completely changed how I ~ now.
vocab.desc: 〜の仕方が完全に変わりました。ある体験・作品が自分に与えた影響を伝えるときの自然な表現。

vocab.phrase: It threw me off at first, but ~
vocab.desc: 最初は戸惑ったけど〜。throw off は戸惑わせるの意味。第一印象と後の評価が違うときに使える。
完全にハマってしまいました。夢中になった・引き込まれたときに使うカジュアルな表現。

vocab.phrase: It depends on who you ask.
vocab.desc: 聞く人によって違います。意見が分かれるテーマで一概には言えないと伝えるときの便利な一言。

vocab.phrase: Off the top of their head
vocab.desc: 何も調べずに・すぐに思いつきで。知識や記憶をその場でさっと出せるときに使う表現。

vocab.phrase: It keeps you on your toes.
vocab.desc: 気が抜けない・油断できない。次に何が起こるかわからないスリルや緊張感を表すイディオム。
難しいけど、あえて言うなら〜。迷いながらも答えを選ぶときの自然な言い回し。

title: Music Talk
subtitle: City Pop, Jazz Bars & Japanese Music

dialogue.speaker: Haruki
dialogue.text: You were humming something earlier. Was that a Japanese song?

dialogue.speaker: Lena
dialogue.text: Ha, you caught me! It was Plastic Love by Mariya Takeuchi. I've been obsessed with city pop lately.

dialogue.speaker: Haruki
dialogue.text: No way! That song had such a crazy resurgence online. I was surprised how many overseas fans discovered it through YouTube.

dialogue.speaker: Dev
dialogue.text: Same for me. The algorithm just dropped it on me one night and I went down a rabbit hole for hours. Tatsuro Yamashita, Anri — I couldn't stop.

dialogue.speaker: Lena
dialogue.text: There's something about the production — that warm, slightly retro sound. It feels nostalgic for a time and place I've never actually lived in.

dialogue.speaker: Haruki
dialogue.text: That's exactly how a lot of Japanese people feel too. It captures a kind of 80s optimism that people look back on fondly, even if they weren't alive then.

dialogue.speaker: Dev
dialogue.text: Do people here still listen to it, or is it more of a nostalgia thing for older generations?

dialogue.speaker: Haruki
dialogue.text: Both, honestly. Younger people have rediscovered it, partly thanks to the internet. There are jazz bars in Tokyo where they play this kind of music all night.

dialogue.speaker: Lena
dialogue.text: Wait — jazz bars? That is going on my itinerary immediately. Can you recommend one?

dialogue.speaker: Haruki
dialogue.text: Sure! There's a great one in Shimokitazawa. Very low-key, vinyl records only, cash only. It's the kind of place where nobody talks too loud.

dialogue.speaker: Dev
dialogue.text: I love that. Sometimes the best experiences are the ones you stumble into with no expectations. This whole trip has been like that.

dialogue.speaker: Lena
dialogue.text: Totally. And music is such a good gateway into a culture. I feel like I understand something about Japan better just from listening to city pop.

dialogue.speaker: Haruki
dialogue.text: That might be the most thoughtful thing anyone has said to me about Japanese music. I'll make you both a playlist tonight.

vocab.phrase: I went down a rabbit hole.
vocab.desc: どんどん深みにはまってしまいました。何かに夢中になって次々と調べ続ける状態を表す定番表現。

vocab.phrase: Nostalgic for a time I've never lived in
vocab.desc: 経験したことのない時代への郷愁。自分が生まれる前の時代や場所に懐かしさを感じるときに使える詩的な表現。
〜を懐かしく振り返る。過去の出来事や時代を温かい気持ちで思い出すときに使う。

vocab.phrase: That is going on my itinerary immediately.
vocab.desc: すぐに予定に入れます！誰かの提案にすごく乗り気なときの即断的な返し方。
落ち着いた・控えめな・派手じゃない。雰囲気やスタイルを説明するときのカジュアルな形容表現。
〜であるような場所。場所の雰囲気や特徴を具体的に説明するときに使える便利な構文。
何も期待せずに偶然出会う。予想外に良い体験をしたときの言い方。
〜への入り口・きっかけ。何かを理解するための入口になるものを表すときに使う比喩的な表現。

title: Work & Life Balance
subtitle: Remote Work, Career & Japanese Work Culture

dialogue.speaker: Yui
dialogue.text: Can I ask you something? You both work remotely from different countries. Do you ever feel like you're missing out on office culture?

dialogue.speaker: Marcus
dialogue.text: Honestly, sometimes. The spontaneous conversations over coffee — you can't really replicate that on a video call. But I've stopped feeling guilty about it.

dialogue.speaker: Chloe
dialogue.text: Same. I think remote work taught me to be much more intentional about communication. You can't rely on osmosis anymore — you have to actually reach out.

dialogue.speaker: Yui
dialogue.text: That's interesting. Here, there's still a strong culture of being physically present — staying late, going out with your team like this. It's slowly changing, but old habits die hard.

dialogue.speaker: Marcus
dialogue.text: Do you feel pressure to stay late even when the work is done?

dialogue.speaker: Yui
dialogue.text: Sometimes. There's a word for it — karoshi — overwork to the point of burnout. It's a real issue and people are more aware of it now, but the pressure is still there.

dialogue.speaker: Chloe
dialogue.text: We have a version of that too — hustle culture. The idea that if you're not grinding, you're falling behind. I had to actively unlearn that mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Marcus
dialogue.text: What helped me was getting clear on what I actually want from my career, not just what looks impressive. Easier said than done, though.

dialogue.speaker: Yui
dialogue.text: I think being here tonight, talking like this — it's a reminder that work is one part of life, not the whole thing. That's easy to forget in the day-to-day.

dialogue.speaker: Chloe
dialogue.text: Exactly. And ironically, I do some of my best thinking when I step away from my desk. This trip has already given me three new ideas.

dialogue.speaker: Marcus
dialogue.text: That's the thing about getting out of your routine. New inputs lead to new outputs.

dialogue.speaker: Yui
dialogue.text: I like that. Okay — enough about work. We're at an izakaya. Let's order one more round and actually switch off for the rest of the night.

dialogue.speaker: Chloe
dialogue.text: Now that is the best idea anyone has had all evening.

vocab.desc: 〜を逃している・体験できていない。FOMO（取り残される不安）を表すときにもよく使われる表現。

vocab.phrase: I've stopped feeling guilty about it.
vocab.desc: それに罪悪感を感じるのをやめました。以前は気にしていたが、今は受け入れているというニュアンスの成長表現。
〜に対して意識的・目的を持って取り組む。なんとなくではなく、きちんと考えて行動するときに使う。
昔の習慣はなかなか変わらない。慣れ親しんだやり方や文化がすぐには変わらないことを表すことわざ。
言うは易く行うは難し。理論ではわかっていても実践は難しいというときの定番フレーズ。
〜を意識的に忘れる・手放す。間違って覚えたことや不要になった考え方を捨てる努力を表す。

vocab.phrase: New inputs lead to new outputs.
vocab.desc: 新しい刺激が新しい発想を生む。環境を変えることの大切さを表すシンプルで覚えやすい表現。
仕事モードをオフにする・気持ちを切り替える。物理的なスイッチのように頭を休ませるときに使う比喩表現。
